content.problemStatement:
From an array AAA containing NNN integers, you construct a binary string SSS of length (N−1)(N - 1)(N−1) as follows. For all 1≤i<N1 \le i \lt N1≤i<N:


If Ai<Ai+1A_i < A_{i+1}Ai​<Ai+1​, then Si=0S_i = 0Si​=0.
If Ai>Ai+1A_i > A_{i+1}Ai​>Ai+1​, then Si=1S_i = 1Si​=1.
Given the string SSS, determine the count of indices iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) such that it is possible for AiA_iAi​ to be the **maximum** element of the array AAA.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains the binary string SSS of length (N−1)(N - 1)(N−1) containing 000s and 111s only.


Output Format
For each test case, output the count of indices iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) such that it is possible for AiA_iAi​ to be the **maximum** element of the array AAA.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
S contains only '0' and '1' and has length N‑1
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5), the size of the array. The second line contains a binary string S of length N‑1 consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the number of indices i (1 ≤ i ≤ N) that can be the maximum element of the array consistent with the given string S.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we count the positions that are peaks (left neighbour not larger and right neighbour not smaller). The counts are 1, 1 and 2 respectively, matching the sample.

solution.approach:
For an index i to possibly hold the global maximum we must not have a forced larger element on either side. From the definition of S, S_i = 0 means A_i < A_{i+1} (so i cannot be maximum), and S_i = 1 means A_i > A_{i+1} (so i+1 cannot be maximum). Therefore i can be maximum iff:
- i is the first element or the comparison with its left neighbour is not a strict decrease, i.e., (i == 1) or S_{i‑2} == '0'.
- i is the last element or the comparison with its right neighbour is not a strict increase, i.e., (i == N) or S_{i‑1} == '1'.
These two conditions describe exactly the “peaks” of the up‑down pattern. We simply scan the string once and count positions satisfying both conditions. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, overall O(sum of N) ≤ 10^5

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        S = next(it)
        ans = 0
        for i in range(1, N+1):
            left_ok = (i == 1) or (S[i-2] == '0')
            right_ok = (i == N) or (S[i-1] == '1')
            if left_ok and right_ok:
                ans += 1
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
